coredata.dc:title: Prepared carbon dots from wheat straw for detection of Cu2+ in cells and zebrafish and room temperature phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Jiahui
Zhou, Yunhao
Ning, Juan
Hu, Guizhen
Zhang, Qingyou
Hou, Yabin
Zhou, Yanmei
coredata.dc:description: The green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from biomass is critical for their sustainable application. Herein, using wheat straw as a single precursor, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared through a one-step carbonization process, and the obtained CDs have intense blue luminescence and excitation-independent photoluminescent behavior. The solution of CDs shows good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity successfully used as hopeful bioimaging and biosensing probe for Cu2+ in HepG2 cells and zebrafish. Based on CDs, boron-doped carbon dots with IPA shells (CDs@IPA) can be obtained by doping boron element and isophthalic acid (IPA) coating. CDs@IPA irradiated with different wavelength ultraviolet lamps shows different solid fluorescence, while turning off the ultraviolet lamp can produce green visible room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) to the naked eyes for 5 s. The two kinds of wheat straw-based carbon dots have bifunctional luminescence properties and can be used to detect Cu2+ and serve as RTP anti-counterfeiting signs to ensure information security.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wheat straw
Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Biosensing
Information security

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to Cu2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21978069 , 21875061 , 21975066 ), Henan Province Science and Technology Research Project (222102520026), and Open Research Fund Program of Science and Technology on Aerospace Chemical Power Laboratory (120201B01).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Survey of XPS of CDs. (b-d) The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s, respectively.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra (λex = 350 nm) of the CDs in aqueous solution; (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution under different excitation wavelengths range from 330 nm to 380 nm.

(a) Fluorescence absorption spectra of the CDs solution in the presence and absence of Cu2+; (b) Selectivity and interference of CDs solution to different metal ions (200 μM). 1-blank, 2-Cu2+, 3-Zn2+, 4-Al3+, 5-Ni+, 6-Hg2+, 7-Li+, 8-Fe3+, 9-Cr6+, 10-Ag+, 11-Mn2+, 12-Co2+, 13-Fe2+, 14-Ba2+, 15-Pb2+; (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs solution on addition of Cu2+(0–200 μM); inset: the linear relationship between F/F0 and Cu2+; (d) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs in aqueous solution with and without Cu2+ ions.

Confocal images of HepG2 cells incubated with the CDs and Cu2+ of (a) 0 μM, (b) 50 μM, (c) 100 μM, (d) 150 μM, (e) 200 μM; (f) Relative fluorescence intensity of HepG2 cells measured by Image J. Date is presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Scale bar = 20 μm.

Inverted fluorescence microscope imaging of Cu2+ in zebrafish. (a-d) Images of the CDs with the different concentrations of Cu2+ (0, 50, 150, 200 μM); (e) Relative fluorescence intensity of zebrafish measured by Image J. Date is presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Scale bar = 500 μm.

(a) Fluorescence images of CDs@IPA under the excitation of 254 and 365 nm UV light, and phosphorescence image after ceasing the excitation of 254 and 365 nm UV light for 1–7 s. (b) Images of information encryption by B-CDs and CDs@IPA.
Schematic illustration of the preparation and application of CDs and CDs@IPA from wheatstraw.

head.ce:title: Prepared carbon dots from wheat straw for detection of Cu2+ in cells and zebrafish and room temperature phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from biomass is critical for their sustainable application. Herein, using wheat straw as a single precursor, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared through a one-step carbonization process, and the obtained CDs have intense blue luminescence and excitation-independent photoluminescent behavior. The solution of CDs shows good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity successfully used as hopeful bioimaging and biosensing probe for Cu2+ in HepG2 cells and zebrafish. Based on CDs, boron-doped carbon dots with IPA shells (CDs@IPA) can be obtained by doping boron element and isophthalic acid (IPA) coating. CDs@IPA irradiated with different wavelength ultraviolet lamps shows different solid fluorescence, while turning off the ultraviolet lamp can produce green visible room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) to the naked eyes for 5 s. The two kinds of wheat straw-based carbon dots have bifunctional luminescence properties and can be used to detect Cu2+ and serve as RTP anti-counterfeiting signs to ensure information security.

ce:section.ce:para: As a fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have the characteristics of high resistance to photobleaching, simple synthesis, and stable emission [1–3], and have been widely used in detection, medical imaging, catalysis, chemical analysis and other fields [4–14]. In recent years, carbon dots had been used to detect heavy metals, such as Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+ [3,8,15] because excess mental ions exist in the environment or human body, which will produce varying degrees of damage. For example, excess Cu2+ can promote the production of reactive oxygen species, which can lead to neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease [8,16].The world produces a large amount of biomass disposal of biomass every year, but most of them had not been properly treated. However, biomass itself not only provides an abundant source of carbon but also minimizes the use of chemicals. Therefore, a serious of studies on the preparation of CDs using biomass such as watermelon [17], flax straw [18], corn husk [19], and orange peel [20] as green carbon sources has appeared as a solution.

Besides metal ion sensing, CDs can also be applied to room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) [21–23]. RTP materials have attracted extensive attention for their excellent performance in chemical sensing [24], biological imaging [25–27] and anti-counterfeiting [28–31]. In general, CDs does not produce phosphorescence due to unstable triplet excited states. To achieve efficient RTP for CDs, the manufacturing method needs to satisfy several key characteristics and principles. Firstly, it is vital for heteroatomic doping (B, N, Cl, etc.) to produce hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds for reducing the energy gap difference between singlet and triplet states. Secondly, build a highly rigid network to isolate the CDs from external quenching factors. As currently reported, only a small of carbon dots synthesized with biomass as precursors exhibit ultra-long room-temperature phosphorescence. Huang [32] used spinach as precursor and added boric acid to prepare phosphorescent carbon dots for generating hydrogen bond cross-linking network, but it took a long time to prepare CDs. Liu [33,34] purified silica from rice husks and calcined it in situ form carbon dots to realize ultra-long lifetimes RTP. However, the multi-step acid-base treatment makes the steps complicated and the operation is relatively difficult. Although some progress has been made in the preparation of phosphorescence CDs, it is still necessary to expand the selection of raw materials and simplify the operation steps. Wheat straw contains a small amount of silicon, and it has been reported that the doping of silicon was contributed to create covalent bonds. Compared to weak hydrogen bonds, covalent bonds can be employed as a much more effective alternative to fix and rigidify triplet emission species. At the same time, wheat straw contains other heteroatoms could generate hydrogen bonds.[33,35,36].

Herein, high fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by one-step carbonization of wheat straw. The non-fluorescent compounds form by the strong coordination interaction between the rich surface functional groups of the CDs and Cu2+. The performance of CDs has been verified in liver cancer HepG2 cells and zebrafish, and with the addition of different concentrations of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity in cells and zebrafish decreased gradually. As shown in scheme 1 , room temperature phosphorescence occurs when the boron element is doped on the CDs and coated with IPA, which realizes the doping of heteroatoms and isolating from contact with air and water. In addition, under the confinement of the IPA shell, the distance between the internal B-CDs is shrunk while the forces such as hydrogen bonds are enhanced, so that the RTP can be observed by the naked eyes at about 5 s. The strategy takes wheat straw as raw material through simple processing to achieve fluorescence detection of Cu2+ and phosphorescence information encryption is meaningful.

All chemicals are analytical reagents and are used without further purification. Wheat straw comes from a farm in Kaifeng, Henan Province. Boric acid, sodium tetraborate, isophthalic acid, zinc chloride, aluminum chloride, nickel nitrate, mercury sulfate, lithium sulfate, ferrous sulfate, ferric chloride, potassium permanganate, silver chloride, manganese sulfate, cobalt nitrate, barium chloride, copper sulfate, and lead nitrate were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Ultrapure water comes from the chemical laboratory. Fluorescence and absorption spectra were collected on Edinburgh FS5 and U-4100, respectively. Cell images were taken on a confocal microscope (Olympus FV3000). Nikon Ti2-U inverted fluorescence microscope was used for zebrafish imaging. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was recorded on JEM-2100. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy were recorded on Bruker VERTEX 70. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted by Escalab 250Xi electron spectrometer. The fluorescent lifetime was measured by Edinburgh FLS980. X-ray diffraction spectrum (XRD) was conducted by Bruker Apex II.

3.71 g of wheat straw was first carbonized in the air at 250℃ for 2 h and the yield was 58.47%. Added 20 mL ultrapure water to 0.15 g carbonized wheat straw and ultrasonic for 10 min to fully mix the solution. Then, filtering with a 220 nm filter and a light-yellow solution can be obtained after filtration. Then the solution was transferred into the dialysis bag (MWCO = 500 Da) for 24 h to remove the remaining small molecules and freeze-drying to obtain CDs powder.

CDs powder was dissolved in ultrapure water to obtain CDs solution (1.2 mg/mL), and then added different metal ions solutions (Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Li+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr6+, Ag+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Pb2+) to ensure that the final working volume is constant to 2.5 mL. The fluorescence spectra were recorded by Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer at 350 nm excitation. For actual samples analysis, the lake water was obtained from a lake at Henan University after being filtered by a 220 nm filter three times.

The cytotoxicity of CDs in HepG2 cells was assessed using the MTT test and a 96-plate was used to disseminate the cells. In each well, HepG2 cells were incubated in 10% fetal bovine serum and different concentrations of CDs (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 mg/ mL) were injected at 37℃ with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Carefully removed suck the supernatant and replace it with 90 μLof fresh culture medium, followed by 10 μLof MTT solution, and cultivate for another 4 h. Removed the supernatant and add 110 μLFormazan solution to each well. An enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay was conducted for the absorbance value of each hole at 490 nm.

HepG2 cells were incubated in 10% fetal bovine serum at 37℃ for 4 h with various concentrations of Cu2+ (0, 50, 100, 150, 200 μM). PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) was used to wash the cells three times. The cells were then cultivated with 5 mg/mL CDs at 37℃ for 12 h, the leftover CDs were washed with PBS, and confocal imaging was used to record the fluorescence images. To evaluate the practical applicability of CDs as a fluorescent probe in the living organism, 4-day-old wild-type zebrafish were treated with different concentrations of Cu2+ after feeding at 28℃ for 15 min. Zebrafish were then washed three times with PBS and fed with 5 mg/mL CDs solution for 30 min and then dropped anesthetics on zebrafish. The imaging of zebrafish was recorded with an inverted fluorescence microscope.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the CDs from wheat straw in Fig. 1 are nearly spherical and completely dispersed with an average particle size of 1.73 nm. The XRD pattern of CDs in Figure S1a exhibited a diffuse peak centered at 2θ = 24.5°, indicating the amorphous nature of the prepared CDs[37]. In the FT-IR spectra of CDs (Figure S2b), the stretching vibration with peaks at 3490 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H[5], and the characteristic peak at 2985 cm−1 coincides with the stretching vibration of C-H[38]. The band at 1650, 1480, and 1290 cm−1 can be contributed to the C = N, C = C, and C-O[18]. The above FT-IR results indicate that there are some functional groups such as –OH, –NH2, and –COOH on the surface of the obtained CDs.

The XPS spectra were examined to identify the elemental composition and surface functional groups of the CDs. The spectrum of the CDs in Fig. 2 showed that there were three main elements of CDs: 50.17% carbon, 32.01% oxygen, and 2.81% nitrogen. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s had three major peaks at 284.2, 285.8, and 287.6 eV, which associated to C = C/C-C, C-O, and C = O, respectively [5], and it is consistent with the FT-IR results. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s had three main peaks, located at 531.6, and 532.4 eV, corresponding to C = O, and C-O, respectively [4]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s had three primary peaks, situated at 398.4, 399.6, and 400.2 eV, associating with N-B, N-C, and N-H, respectively [35].

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to determine the optical characteristics of the CDs. The solution of CDs displays an absorption peak at 256 nm, which is attributed to π − π* transitions of the C = C bond [23], as seen in Fig. 3 a, which illustrates the optimal excitation and emission of CDs at 350 nm and 434 nm, respectively. In Fig. 3b, when the excitation wavelengths increase from 330 nm to 380 nm, the peak value of fluorescence emission almost remains unchanged (λem = 434 nm). The excitation-independent emission characteristics show that the surface of CDs is almost uniform [39].

The effects of various pH, time, temperatures, and salt concentrations of CDs on their fluorescence intensity were investigated to better understand the fluorescence stability. The fluorescence intensity of CDs drops only 3.6% under 120 min of continuous UV light (Figure S2a), demonstrating that the CDs are photobleaching resistant. When the temperature of the CDs solution increased from 10 to 70℃ (Figure S2b), the fluorescence stability remained well, which indicates that the CDs can work properly at different temperatures. Figure S2c exhibits that the fluorescence intensity changed mildly with increasing concentration of NaCl (0–1 M), and indicates that CDs possessed satisfactory stability. Under different acid-base conditions, the fluorescence of CDs alters in varying degrees (Figure S2d). At the pH range of 4–8, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was negligible, and under strong acid-base conditions, the decrease of fluorescence intensity could be caused by protonation and deprotonation of functional groups (–OH/–COOH and –NH2) on the surface of CDs [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption peaks without Cu2+ of CDs around 270 nm disappears and the absorption peak with Cu2+ at 280 nm gradually enhanced, demonstrating that Cu2+ indeed complexed with CDs to form non-fluorescent materials [41]. Fig. 4 b shows the selectivity of CDs to Cu2+ and the interference of fluorescence response to different metal ions. Except for Cu2+, F/F0 hardly changed (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CDs before and after adding metal ions at 434 nm respectively). The fluorescence intensity of CDs steadily declined with increasing Cu2+ concentration from 0 to 200 μM under 350 nm excitation, as displayed in Fig. 4c. The F/F0 exhibited a good linear with the concentration of Cu2+(inset in Fig. 4c), and the limit of detections (LOD) of Cu2+ is estimated to be 3.23 μM (LOD = 3Sd/k, Sd: standard deviation of blank solution; k: the slope of the standard curve). The Stern-Volmer equation might be used to investigate whether the mechanism of this “on-off” fluorescence probe is static or dynamic quenching.
F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q] = 1 + Kqτ0 [Q],

Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs solution without and with Cu2+ at the optimum excitation wavelength of 350 nm, respectively. Kq is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Ksv is the slope of the linear regression equation between F0/F and the concentration of Cu2+, τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, and [Q] is the concentration of Cu2+. After calculation the Kq is 6.48 × 1014 M−1s−1, this Kq value is much larger than the maximal collision quenching constant reported (2.0 × 1010 M−1s−1) [18]. To further explain the mechanism of Cu2+ quenching CDs, Fig. 4d exhibits the fluorescence lifetime decays of CDs in an aqueous solution. The findings display that the fluorescence decay curves were best fitted to a double exponential decay with the fluorescence lifetimes of 2.62 ns, while after adding Cu2+ the fluorescence lifetimes changed to 2.47 ns. Thus, with the Stern-Volmer quenching constant is larger than the maximal collision quenching and the fluorescence lifetime of CDs remained unchanged before and after the addition of Cu2+, indicating that Cu2+ quenched CDs is a static quenching process [9,18].

To evaluate the utility of the proposed fluorescence sensor for detecting Cu2+, experiments were carried out in lake water samples. As demonstrated in Table S1, the recoveries of Cu2+ in lake and tap water ranged from 97.42 to 106.88%, indicating that biomass-based CDs can be successfully applied to the detection of Cu2+ in real water samples.

To investigate the biological application of the CDs, we carried out living cells and organism imaging experiments. MTT assay (Figure S3) was used to determine the cytotoxicity of CDs, and to explore their value in biomedical applications. Cell viability remained above 95% with increasing CDs concentration (0–5 mg/mL), indicating that this fluorescent probe holds promise for vitro and vivo imaging. HepG2 cells were incubated with CDs (Fig. 5 a) for 24 h at 37℃, and blue fluorescence was noticed in the cells. After that, they were cultivated with various Cu2+ concentrations, it can be seen in cells imaging (Fig. 5b-e) that with the increasing concentration of Cu2+, the blue fluorescence intensity in cells gradually decreases. The results of the experiment illustrated that the CDs could be successfully used to monitoring Cu2+ in the cells. As exhibited in Fig. 6 a-d, imaging of in zebrafish demonstrated that the variation trend of fluorescence intensity appeared opposite to the changing of concentration of Cu2+, which suggested that CDs could be applied to sense Cu2+ in vivo. The histogram of relative fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5f, and Fig. 6e) can directly demonstrate the degree of Cu2+ quenching CDs.

Based on CDs, RTP under ultraviolet lamp irradiation can be obtained by the boron atom doping and coated with IPA. As compared with the FT-IR spectrum (Figure S4a) of IPA, CDs@IPA has a peak that exactly corresponds to the peak of IPA, illustrating the fine crystals that have been produced from IPA via the oil bath heating process[42]. Furthermore, comparable XRD patterns are seen from CDs@IPA and IPA (Figure S4b), the intensities of several X-ray diffraction peaks of CDs@IPA are discovered to be greater than those of IPA. It is likely that inserting B-CDs into IPA crystals would have a little effect on the stacking and crystallization orientation of IPA molecules. [43] The XPS of B-CDs and CDs@IPA were exhibited in Figure S5, and Figure S6 respectively. Comparing with the B-CDs, the survey spectrum of the CDs@IPA showed that there were only two main elements: 67.81% carbon and 32.19% oxygen. This results once again demonstrated the formation of IPA crystal around B-CDs.

The SEM of CDs@IPA displays a polyhedral morphology in Figure S7, it indicates that a three-dimensional shell structure is formed. RTP occurs owning to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the carboxyl groups in IPA and the amino groups in B-CDs, which makes the polyhedral shell firmly wrapped around B-CDs to isolate air and water. Such a series of operations would reduce the transition of non-radiative energy and make the triplet excited state stable, resulting in visible room-temperature phosphorescence. As shown in Fig. 7 a, when the solid powder is illuminated with different UV wavelengths, it shows white (λex = 254 nm) and purple (λex = 365 nm) solid-state fluorescence respectively, and this phenomenon declared that CDs@IPA has multiple light-emitting centers[44]. When the UV lamp turned off, CDs@IPA displays bright green phosphorescence for about 5 s. In the abbreviation of Henan University (HENU), H and N are designed with CDs@IPA, E and U were prepared with B-CDs (Fig. 7b). Bright green phosphorescence can be observed in H and N after 1 s UV irradiation, while no phosphorescence is observed in E and U. The above demonstrations convincingly show that CDs@IPA can be potentially used as materials in anti-counterfeiting fields.

In conclusion, a blue photoluminescent material based on CDs was prepared by a simplified carbonization method using wheat straw. The as-prepared CDs possess abundant surface functional groups, which could selectively recognize Cu2+ and serve as imaging probe for HepG2 cells and zebrafish. By boron-doped CDs improvement and adding a layer of rigid matrix, the triplet excited state of CDs@IPA is stabilized, resulting in better and more pronounced phosphorescence. By irradiating CDs@IPA solid powder with UV lamps of different wavelengths show solid fluorescence of different colors, and the green RTP of about 5 s can be seen by naked eyes, which expands the biological and commercialize application of CDs.

Jiahui Shi: Conceptualization. Yunhao Zhou: Formal analysis. Juan Ning: Formal analysis. Guizhen Hu: Software, Investigation. Qingyou Zhang: Validation, Funding acquisition. Yabin Hou: Supervision, Project administration. Yanmei Zhou: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978069, 21875061, 21975066), Henan Province Science and Technology Research Project (222102520026), and Open Research Fund Program of Science and Technology on Aerospace Chemical Power Laboratory (120201B01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121597.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidative Stress in Neurodegenerative Diseases
